goal: Evaluate buying an electric car with emphasis on reliability for a daily commute, low ongoing costs, and long software/hardware relevance to minimize obsolescence.
Compare total cost of ownership over five years for electric vs gasoline-powered options, including fuel/electric costs, maintenance, insurance, and depreciation.
Incorporate battery degradation expectations and warranty terms into the five-year ownership decision.
Assess retrofit viability and cost-effectiveness of upgrading technology features via retrofit vs upgrading trim over a five-year horizon.
Incorporate public charging time costs and membership fees into five-year total cost of ownership analysis.
constraints: Minimize obsolescence / long software updates and resale value
Daily driving ~40 miles on workdays; real-world range ~200–250 miles; charging convenience matters
Budget-conscious / price sensitivity in model selection
Five-year horizon for total cost of ownership analysis
Battery health and warranty terms should be considered when evaluating five-year TCO and model choices
Trade-in or private sale influence on down payment and financing terms
Software/OTA obsolescence risk prioritized in model selection
Retrofit feasibility, warranty implications, and potential impact on resale
Account for time costs when using public charging and include membership fees in five-year total cost of ownership analysis.
Refundable deposit can be used as negotiation leverage
decisions: Plan for Level-2 charger installation: decide between NEMA 14-50 receptacle vs hardwired charger and update the plan accordingly.
Specify a 60A circuit for the charger to allow up to ~48A charging and future-proofing.
Require formal panel load calculation to determine whether a service upgrade is needed.
Obtain 2–3 written quotes that include permit fees, scope, timeline, and exclusions.
Confirm permit and inspection are included in the installation quote.
Explore rebates/incentives and have installer assist with paperwork.
Check HOA rules early and obtain written approval if required.
Keep permit receipts and inspection records to support resale if selling within a few years.
open: Permits, inspections, and permit fees expectations
HOA approval processes and restrictions and potential timing
Continuous-load rule and 80% circuit sizing considerations
Future-proofing the circuit for potential second EV
Conduit routing, distance from panel, and trenching considerations
Timeline for permit approvals and utility service upgrades
Incentives, rebates, and installer assistance with paperwork
Practical quotes checklist: include formal load calculation, panel upgrade assessment, scope, timeline, exclusions
Steps to avoid common installation surprises
Keep documentation and receipts for resale
Negotiation levers available with a refundable deposit: price/OTD, incentives, trade-in, financing terms, add-ons, delivery timing, demo/loaner pricing
Evaluate value of extended warranty plans for EVs
